title: Anxiety
lead: Anxiety is your body's alarm system doing its job too often, too loudly. It's exhausting — and it's also one of the most treatable experiences there is.
explanation: Anxiety is the feeling of worry, dread, or fear about what might happen. In small doses it's useful — it sharpens focus and keeps us safe. It becomes a problem when the alarm keeps ringing even when there's no real danger, or won't switch off afterwards.

Physically, anxiety is your nervous system shifting into 'fight or flight': faster heartbeat, shallow breathing, tense muscles, a churning stomach. These sensations are uncomfortable but not dangerous — they're your body preparing to protect you.

You are not broken, weak, or 'too sensitive'. Anxiety disorders are among the most common health conditions in the world, and they respond very well to skills you can learn and support you can reach for.
symptoms: Constant worry that's hard to control

immediate.title: Physiological sigh
steps: Take a normal breath in through your nose
Add a second, shorter sip of air on top to fully inflate your lungs
Let it all out slowly through your mouth with a long exhale

immediate.title: Name it to tame it
immediate.description: Silently label what's happening: 'This is anxiety. It's uncomfortable, not dangerous. It will pass.' Naming the feeling helps your thinking brain come back online.

longTerm.title: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT)
longTerm.description: The most researched approach for anxiety. It teaches you to notice anxious thoughts, test them, and gradually face what you've been avoiding. Many free CBT tools and courses exist.

longTerm.title: Regular movement
longTerm.description: Even a daily walk measurably lowers baseline anxiety by discharging stress hormones and regulating the nervous system.

longTerm.title: Reducing avoidance
longTerm.description: Anxiety shrinks the more you approach — in small, planned steps — the things you fear, rather than avoiding them. This is the core of exposure practice.

resources.title: Anxiety Disorders — overview
resources.whyHere: Clear, science-based primer from a leading public research institute.

resources.whyHere: Free downloadable CBT booklets on anxiety, panic and worry.

resources.whyHere: Completely free, research-backed meditation and awareness training.

resources.title: How to make stress your friend
resources.whyHere: Reframes the physical feelings of anxiety in an empowering, evidence-informed way.

matchPhrases: i feel nervous all the time
i feel scared for no reason
